title: The Resistance
performanceTarget: top 1% execution time and overall performance
note: The C++ folder contains three non-coincident proposals: packed hash DP, trie DP, and sorted-vector DP.

variants.languageReason: C++ packs each Morse encoding into two uint64_t values plus a length, keeps DP counts in native signed 64-bit integers, and performs millions of short hash lookups without substring allocation or managed-runtime overhead.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Selected. It gives exact packed keys, low-overhead unordered_map probes, compact vectors, and straightforward CodinGame I/O.
Considered for raw loops, but implementing a robust custom hash table would increase risk without improving the O(L * 80) DP profile.
Considered for native speed and safety, but custom packed-key hashing and tight scanner loops are less ergonomic on CodinGame and do not improve the constant factors here.
Considered for compiled performance, but map/runtime overhead, bounds checks, and garbage collection are less attractive than C++ primitive storage.
Managed runtimes can solve the puzzle, but VM startup and object/header overhead are weaker for a large dictionary plus millions of lookups.
Useful for correctness mirrors, but interpreter/VM overhead is too high for the largest 100000-by-80 dynamic-programming scans.

variants.approachJustification: Translate every dictionary word to Morse, pack dot/dash bits exactly, count duplicate encodings in a hash table, then run forward word-break DP from each reachable position while extending at most 80 Morse symbols.
variants.timeComplexity: O(N * M + L * B), B <= 80, with expected O(1) dictionary probes
variants.spaceComplexity: O(U + L), where U is the number of distinct Morse encodings

variants.languageReason: C++ stores trie nodes as contiguous structs with two int children and one terminal multiplicity, making each DP extension a direct indexed load rather than a hash-table probe.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Selected. It combines native branch-light trie traversal with automatic vector reservation and compact integer fields.
Considered for minimal abstraction overhead, but manual dynamic node arrays do not materially improve over C++ vectors for this binary trie.
Considered for native speed, but mutable trie construction and indexing add ceremony without a runtime advantage.
Considered for compiled loops, but slice/runtime overhead and bounds checks are less attractive in the trie hot path.
JIT performance is competitive, but array/object layout and startup behavior are weaker than fixed native structs for raw CodinGame speed.
Rejected for the speed objective because trie transitions and DP updates run millions of times.

variants.approachJustification: Insert every encoded word into a binary trie and store duplicate-word counts at terminal nodes. The DP walks trie edges from each reachable Morse position and stops immediately when the current prefix disappears.
variants.timeComplexity: O(N * M + L * B), B <= 80, usually with fewer operations due to early trie breaks
variants.spaceComplexity: O(T + L), where T is the number of trie nodes and T <= total encoded dictionary length + 1

variants.languageReason: C++ represents all dictionary encodings in one flat sorted vector of primitive fields, avoiding hash buckets, trie prefixes, and per-object managed-runtime overhead.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Selected. It keeps a compact contiguous array and uses standard sort plus manual binary search with negligible metadata overhead.
Considered, but it would reproduce the same flat array manually while saving only constant metadata and increasing implementation risk.
Considered for compact vectors, but it does not reduce memory below the C++ primitive-entry layout.
Considered, but runtime overhead and less predictable struct packing are weaker for the memory objective.
Primitive arrays can be compact, but object-free grouped key handling is less natural and VM overhead remains.
Rejected because per-object/per-string overhead dominates the dictionary memory budget.

variants.approachJustification: Store packed Morse keys in a single vector, sort and merge equal encodings, index ranges by encoded length, then run the same DP using binary search inside the relevant length range.
variants.timeComplexity: O(N log N + N * M + L * B * log U), B <= 80
variants.spaceComplexity: O(U + L) with lower constants than hash or trie storage

reasoningSummary: The puzzle is word-break counting over a dot/dash string. Since a dictionary word encodes to at most 80 Morse symbols, dynamic programming over stream positions with incremental dictionary matching gives linear-in-L behavior with a small fixed factor.
timeComplexity: Recommended and runtime: O(N * M + L * B), memory variant: O(N log N + N * M + L * B * log U), B <= 80
spaceComplexity: Recommended: O(U + L), runtime: O(T + L), memory: O(U + L) with smaller constants
